original: So she’s a former space pirate? That must come with a ton of stories. Does she feel like sharing any of them?
“Some of that’s classified,” Monika says, then smirks. “No, not really. Old habit. But if you’ve got time and you want to know, I can share.”
You did ask, and you’re not going anywhere at the moment. So you’re free to stay a while and listen.

“So, to skip over a lot of other stuff,” Monika begins, “my first time as a pirate wasn’t with my old crew. It was with someone else’s old crew.” She leans in a little bit toward you. “A very old crew.”
Had the crew been together for a long time, or were they all elderly, or...?

“Little of both, [pc.name],” she says. “You don’t find many old space pirates. The job’s got a real habit of killing most of us.” She looks a little grim for a moment, then continues. “And when you do find the old ones, you learn they’ve lived that long for a reason.”

Monika looks over your shoulder, peering around the bar, keeping an eye on everything. Faint light flickers in her cybernetic eye, making it look like it’s analyzing whatever she sees. Which seems like a safe guess.

“That crew had probably been at the pirate thing for two or three decades. Most of them were greybeards, a bunch of men who got into the smuggling trade and never learned to do anything else. I thought I could learn something from them.”
That sounds reasonable, but also not like the sort of life most people want when they take up piracy. Did she learn anything from them?

“All the basics,” Monika says. She pulls out another shot glass and starts twirling it around one finger, then makes it dance to another. “Sleight of hand, for one. It’s easier to make people sure you don’t have something if you make it disappear.” Sure enough, she flips her hand over, and the glass seems to disappear. “Basic hacking, how to tail someone and how to lose a tail, ways to read flight patterns and shipment records. I could go on.”

Monika spins her hand again, and sets the shot glass down on the bar, then gives you a hard look. “Real trouble was, those old men didn’t know how to roll with the changes, and still worked like it was thirty or forty years ago. So after a while, I knew I wasn’t going to get anywhere with them. And when-”

A zaika woman with one swollen boob hanging out of her top stumbles up to the bar, just short of crashing into your shoulder as she trips over a barstool. She catches herself with both hands, then leans in toward Monika. The look on her face makes it clear she’s well and truly gone, completely wasted on milk.
“More,” the drunkard murmurs, holding a hand out toward Monika.

“Not a chance, girl,” Monika says, and folds her arms over her chest. “I cut you off two hours ago. If you’re still this gone, you’re not getting another shot.” She glances over to the cowgirl across the bar. “Josie! You heard that, right?”
“Gotcha the first time,” Josie says, looking over her shoulder. “I haven’t given her anything since.”
Monika nods, then glares at the wasted zaika. “Take off,” she says. “Or do you want me to call Karrah?”

RandomInCollection("The zaika woman gives Monika a sullen look, which changes to the most pathetic attempt at puppy-eyes you’ve ever seen. In return, Monika only looks more stern. After a moment, the zaika mutters “Sorry,” and stumbles away. Monika watches her go, a frown on her face.","The zaika woman lets out a long whine, and flails her hands at Monika, mumbling something about milk. Monika raises her hand, brandishing the stunner at the zaika. She doesn’t even have to use it before the zaika backs off with a burbling shriek, then falls on her ass as she tries to back away. She scrambles backward across the floor, somehow making it back to her table. Monika scowls, watching her go.","The zaika woman bares her teeth, and makes a lunge at Monika, catching herself on the bar. Monika leaps backward with ease, and says a single word that you don’t catch. A moment later, Karrah storms in through the bar’s entry and catches the zaika woman around the waist, then hauls her out of the room, kicking and screaming. Monika watches them go, a sullen look on her face.")
“Kind of sad when it gets that bad,” she says, her voice low.
She shakes her head, and pulls another mug from below the bar, then starts polishing it.
You wait a moment for her to continue her story, but it looks like she’s done talking. Maybe if you ask her about this another time, she’ll feel like saying more.
Monika looks at you, and says, “Where was I?”
She was telling you how she wasn’t getting anywhere working with the old man crew. Is that what got her started with the crew she had before coming to Dhaal?

“It was,” Monika says, and the first hints of a smile creep onto her face. “The crew wasn’t all old men, but they were the ones in charge. And a few of us realized we weren’t getting what we wanted. It didn’t look like we had too many options, either.”
Well, they were pirates. Couldn’t they just mutiny?

Monika fixes her gaze on you, both her organic and cybernetic eyes staring you down with equal intensity. After a moment, she laughs, just a little. “We could have,” she says. “But those old codgers, they were big on the whole ‘honor among thieves’ thing, and they made us want to work that way too. It’s probably why they’d been pirates for so long and never had a mutiny in the first place.”
Your experience with space pirates has varied a lot, but she’s got a point. Some of them are almost honorable, others are outright scum. But they’ve all got some kind of their own code.

“There is a code,” Monika says with a nod, and it sounds like she believes it. “The old men taught us, and we stuck to it. So stabbing them in the back wasn’t on the table. Which meant a few of us girls had to form our own group if we wanted out. So we did.”

Monika pulls out a narrow glass and starts polishing it, then holds it up to the light. “It started with four of us. We had dozens of successful missions under our belts, and we knew we worked well together. And we’d seen how the old men worked, with a gang of five that led them, not just one captain. So we set ourselves up like that.”

She lowers the glass, and looks at you. “Three captains. A gryvain, a rahn, and me. The fourth woman was a human, hell of a pilot but she didn’t want to lead. She just wanted to fly her own ship, and we all understood that. We picked up Karrah right before our first job, which got us our ship and our muscle. And that’s how it all started.”
What did they call themselves? Pirates usually give themselves some kind of name. And she never mentioned the name of the old man crew either.

Monika shakes her head, lowering her eyes. “We don’t use the name anymore,” she says, any trace of mirth gone from her voice. “Not the old men’s name since we left, not the crew’s since we broke up. We left that all behind.”
It sounds like she’s not happy that things ended. How did it all go bad?

She gives you a careful look, then slides the glass she’s holding back under the bar. “That’s a story for another time,” she says. “I try not to think about it too much, not if I want to stay in a good mood.”
It’s pretty clear she’s not going to continue that story right now. Maybe if you ask her about it again after they know you better at the bar, she’ll feel like telling you more.
Monika looks at you, and says, “Where was I?”
She was telling you how she wasn’t getting anywhere working with the old man crew. Is that what got her started with the crew she had before coming to Dhaal?

“It was,” Monika says, and the first hints of a smile creep onto her face. “The crew wasn’t all old men, but they were the ones in charge. And a few of us realized we weren’t getting what we wanted. It didn’t look like we had too many options, either.”
Well, they were pirates. Couldn’t they just mutiny?

Monika fixes her gaze on you, both her organic and cybernetic eyes staring you down with equal intensity. After a moment, she laughs, just a little. “We could have,” she says. “But those old codgers, they were big on the whole ‘honor among thieves’ thing, and they made us want to work that way too. It’s probably why they’d been pirates for so long and never had a mutiny in the first place.”
Your experience with space pirates has varied a lot, but she’s got a point. Some of them are almost honorable, others are outright scum. But they’ve all got some kind of their own code.

“There is a code,” Monika says with a nod, and it sounds like she believes it. “The old men taught us, and we stuck to it. So stabbing them in the back wasn’t on the table. Which meant a few of us girls had to form our own group if we wanted out. So we did.”

Monika pulls out a narrow glass and starts polishing it, then holds it up to the light. “It started with four of us. We had dozens of successful missions under our belts, and we knew we worked well together. And we’d seen how the old men worked, with a gang of five that led them, not just one captain. So we set ourselves up like that.”

She lowers the glass, and looks at you. “Three captains. A gryvain, a rahn, and me. The fourth woman was a human, hell of a pilot but she didn’t want to lead. She just wanted to fly her own ship, and we all understood that. We picked up Karrah right before our first job, which got us our ship and our muscle. And that’s how it all started.”
What did they call themselves? Pirates usually give themselves some kind of name. And she never mentioned the name of the old man crew either.

Monika shakes her head, lowering her eyes. “We don’t use the name anymore,” she says, any trace of mirth gone from her voice. “Not the old men’s name since we left, not the crew’s since we broke up. We left that all behind.”
It sounds like she’s not happy that things ended. How did it all go bad?
She takes a deep breath, then slides the glass she’s holding back under the bar. “Same way all good pirates go,” Monika says after a moment. “Get in too much trouble with someone who’s better.”
That doesn’t sound good. Must have been a lot of trouble for it to get the whole crew to break up.

“It really was,” Monika says. She leans one curvy hip on the bar, and folds her arms over her stomach. Her natural eye falls half-closed as she looks at you. “We had it good for a long time. Crew came and went, but we had a core we could count on. I knew it wouldn’t last forever, but I didn’t think it would end so quickly. All it took was one bad job.”
You’ve seen what happens when pirate work goes wrong. You’ve even made some pirates’ work go wrong during your time as a mercenary, though you don’t mention that part.
Anything can go wrong with one bad job. And you’ve worked enough tech to know how quickly things can go bad, and how much can go wrong before anyone can fix the problem.
You definitely know what that’s like. During your time as a smuggler, you were on both ends of that, and it was never as fun as the stories make it out to be.
You’ve been lucky enough to avoid that kind of experience first-hand, but you’ve seen it repeated a thousand times across media on the extranet. Tight crews have broken up over less.
What happened that got the whole crew to break up?
“Three words,” Monika says, and looks you in the eye. “The Black Void.”
Shit. Yeah, they’ll ruin any crew’s day, if not their entire lives. Assuming the Void even lets anyone live.

Monika nods. “We got lucky, and didn’t run into them directly,” she says. “But we took a hit job where we didn’t know it was their stuff. It was supposed to be simple – shoot, loot, and scoot, like Redd used to say. But all the goods had tracers and Black Void markers in the boxes. We saw those and knew we’d fucked up bad.”
And just ditching the goods probably wouldn’t work. Not if the Void knew who’d robbed them. They’d come looking for revenge.

“We knew that,” Monika says. “I came up with a plan that I thought would get us out safe. The Planet Rush had just started, so we’d ditch the goods on one of the Rush worlds, where there would be tons of people coming and going. We argued a lot about what to do and where to go, but I won out. We dropped the crates off in Myrellion’s spaceport, even left an apology along with the goods. Kind of a shame, we could have done well on that world. But the Void got to us before we could get out of the system.”
That couldn’t have gone well. Did everyone get out alive?

“Barely. We got to the warp gate, but the ship was hardly holding together. We knew she only had one more run in her, and Dhaal was the other planet we thought would be a good place to ditch the goods. So we made this our last port.” Monika looks away for a moment. “And that was too much for most of the crew.”

She gestures at the bar. “[flags.MASTODON_END|You know the rest of the story. But thanks to you, it looks like we’re not going to be stuck here forever.|So now we’re here, with what’s left of the crew and our ship, until we can figure out what to do next.]” Monika gives you a smile, [flags.MASTODON_END|a genuine one that reaches her organic eye|though it’s clearly forced, and doesn’t reach her eyes]. “Kind of [flags.MASTODON_END|looking forward to being a pirate again, you know what I mean?|makes you rethink being a pirate, doesn’t it?]”
You[flags.MASTODON_END| can see why she’s ready to get back to her old life|’ve got other plans for your future], that’s for sure. Especially after a hell of a story like that.
You ask Monika what she can tell you about the bar. It’s definitely an unusual place, and you’ve been to a lot of bars on your travels.

Monika glances around, then gives you a wry smile. “I swear, [pc.name], I wake up here sometimes and I wonder how it happened,” she says, then laughs a little. “I don’t think this is what any of us thought we’d end up doing.”
You can see that. But it seems like they’re doing pretty well here, though getting it started must have had some problems.

“That it did,” she says, and her smile fades. Her tails pull a few shot glasses out from under the bar, and she starts polishing them as she talks. “When we first got here, we didn’t have much. The ship was shot to hell, we were low on credits, and the rest of the crew... it’s better if I don’t go into that.” Her lips twitch as she goes quiet, and it looks like there’s something she’s trying not to say.
But they’re here now, and that has to count for something.

Monika nods. “It’s something,” she says. “When we found the old rail station, we were looking for somewhere to crash. Living in hotels adds up real quick, same with eating in the city. So we needed something new, and we needed it fast.” She raises her gaze again, and gestures to the room around her. “And we found this place.”
All this empty space and nobody was using it? That seems unlikely in a city like this.

“We thought so too, but it was bare as a newborn’s ass,” Monika says. “Locals told me later it was for a railway that never got finished, something about zaibatsu politics.” She shrugs, and rolls her organic eye. “But we figured we could make a home here. Then Kiki said we could fix it up with parts from the ship, if we could get them down here. And we knew how the locals were about milk, especially after those damn fools trying to jump us on the street.”

Monika looks upset, and stops herself before she breaks the shot glass she’s polishing. One of her tails returns it to under the bar while the other raises the glass it’s holding. “But then, we got the idea for the bar.” A smile creeps back onto her face as she looks toward the cowgirl on the other side of the bar. “Josie was all for being our milk supply, and we figured out the rest along the way. And look where we are now.”

Not a bad success story, especially in a city like this one. Before you can ask anything else, one of the local women walks up to the bar and asks Monika for another round of drinks. The two of them chat as Monika pours, and as the zaika woman walks away, Monika smirks at her.
“She’s a regular,” Monika says. “Feels a little weird to say that, but it’s nice to know we’ve got people around who like what we do and won’t try to shoot us if we step out of line.”
With that, Monika leans over to help another customer, the conversation clearly over. Maybe if you ask her about this again when she knows you better, she’d be willing to continue.

That’s something you can say about working at a bar that you can’t about being a pirate. Unless you’re a really well-loved pirate, and even then, there’s always going to be someone who has it out for you. Does she feel like she’s got a good handle on running things here?

Monika leans one curvy hip on the bar as her tail puts away the other shot glass, and thinks for a moment. “I do now,” she says. “But I tell you, it’s damn different from running a pirate crew. You ever try to run a business?”

There are so many things you can’t say about that. This is definitely not the place to tell anyone you’re the soon-to-be head of a major industrial company and you’re on the Rush to earn your place there. That’s the kind of thing that could get you kidnapped or worse in this city. So you tell Monika that yes, you know a little about running a business, just things you’ve picked up along the way. She seems satisfied with that.

“So you know some of what I had to deal with,” Monika says. She huffs out a breath. “And this place’s net isn’t the same as the rest of the galaxy’s, so I had to learn how they do everything around here. Never had to do so much stuff in person before, just because I didn’t know how the locals handled things.”
That couldn’t have been too easy, especially since she must have been trying not to attract the zaibatsu’s attention.

“Little bit, yeah,” Monika says, holding two fingers as far apart as she can. “I knew nothing good would come from having their eyes on us. Didn’t exactly make it easy for us to get started, knowing that anyone might be looking to sell us out for their corporate masters.” She snaps you a grin that disappears as quickly as it comes. “But I had two things that most people on this planet weren’t ready for.”
And those were?

“Credits and connections.” Monika puts her hands together, then twists them around each other, and two credit chits appear between her fingers from out of nowhere. “Right after the Rush started up, everybody here wanted that new money. So when we needed some suppliers for the basic bar stuff, we could pay in credits, and that got us a lot farther than any money we made here would. And for people who didn’t want credits....”

Monika gestures to the display of cream liquor behind her, and her smile spreads across her face. “Bonus part of being a pirate is knowing people who can get anything, anywhere. So when you tell them you need all the cream booze they can get as fast as possible, they deliver. And a bottle of something the locals have never tasted... that opens all kinds of doors.”
It sounds like things worked out pretty well for her. For a former pirate, she’s doing a damn good job running a business so far. Do the rest of her crew like it here?
Before Monika can answer, another customer walks up to the bar, waving back and forth where she stands. “I’ll get back to you on that,” Monika says, then turns to the zaika woman.
Looks like someone else has her attention for now. Maybe if you ask her about this again when you’ve spent more time at the bar, you’ll be able to talk more about this.

That’s something you can say about working at a bar that you can’t about being a pirate. Unless you’re a really well-loved pirate, and even then, there’s always going to be someone who has it out for you. Does she feel like she’s got a good handle on running things here?

She seems satisfied with that.

She huffs out a breath. “And this place’s net isn’t the same as the rest of the galaxy’s, so I had to learn how they do everything around here. “Little bit, yeah,” Monika says, holding two fingers as far apart as she can. “I knew nothing good would come from having their eyes on us. Didn’t exactly make it easy for us to get started, knowing that anyone might be looking to sell us out for their corporate masters.” She snaps you a grin that disappears as quickly as it comes. “But I had two things that most people on this planet weren’t ready for.”
And those were?

“Credits and connections.” Monika puts her hands together, then twists them around each other, and two credit chits appear between her fingers from out of nowhere. “Right after the Rush started up, everybody here wanted that new money. So when we needed some suppliers for the basic bar stuff, we could pay in credits, and that got us a lot farther than any money we made here would. And for people who didn’t want credits....”

Monika gestures to the display of cream liquor behind her, and her smile spreads across her face. “Bonus part of being a pirate is knowing people who can get anything, anywhere. So when you tell them you need all the cream booze they can get as fast as possible, they deliver. And a bottle of something the locals have never tasted... that opens all kinds of doors.”
It sounds like things worked out pretty well for her. For a former pirate, she’s doing a damn good job running a business so far. Do the rest of her crew like it here?

Monika nods to the cowgirl on the other side of the bar, who has a row of shot glasses set up, and is filling them two at a time from her nipples, to the cheers of an adoring crowd. “I know Josie’s doing just fine,” she says with a small smile, “but she’s never had a single problem with anything we’ve been through. Must be nice,” she adds quietly, then gives the cowgirl a look you can’t quite read.

After a moment, Monika starts wiping down the bar with her towel, looking every bit the perfect old-fashioned bartender. “I think Karrah misses punching people, and being the bouncer really isn’t enough for her.” She leans in a little closer to you, and lowers her voice. “But I also know she cares more than she wants to let on. Keeping us all safe is the best thing she could be doing for this place, and I know she likes being good at it.”
It does seem like a good role for the thraggen woman. And most people aren’t willing to pick a fight with someone that much larger than them.

“Not down here, at least. The gangs and corps upstairs? That’s different.” She shrugs. “But we do all we can to keep them out. Some of that’s Kiki’s work, and I know she loves what we’ve got going on in here.”
If she’s their techie, then she must have had a lot of work getting this place put together. Refitting an entire ship into a bar isn’t a normal kind of job for most people.

Monika gives you another small smile. “Never seen her so damn excited,” she says. “Putting together this place was probably the biggest thing she’s ever done, and I know it’s her pride and joy. I don’t know if you’ve talked much to her, but she can talk anyone’s ear off about what she did for the bar. Taking apart the ship was hardest for her, but I know it means a lot to Kiki to still have it around in a different way.”
That sounds about right. And what about her?
“Like I said, sometimes I wonder how this all happened,” Monika says, and slings her towel over her shoulder. “Other times, all I can do is look around and be damn glad it’s worked out so well.”

She glances around the bar again. “Think about it. Here we are, in a city like nowhere else in the galaxy, on a planet most people hardly even know about. And we’ve got a bar that’s also like nowhere else, showing people who’ve never left their planet a little bit of what it’s like on other worlds.” She smirks. “And getting them drunk and taking their money at the same time.”
So even if she and the rest of the crew are grounded, they haven’t completely given up being pirates. They’re just letting their marks come to them, instead of going after them.
“That’s a little harsh,” Monika says, though she’s still smiling. “But I’m not saying you’re wrong.”
You ask Monika what she thinks of Dhaal, now that she’s been here for a while and made a home for herself and the crew.

“Easy answer is it could have been a lot worse,” Monika says, then crosses her arms over her chest. “Long story short, we needed to get somewhere far away and a Rush world seemed like our best bet. There weren’t many places we could get to before the ship fell apart.” She shrugs. “Dhaal wouldn’t have been my first choice, but here we are.”
You’d bet a lot of rushers have stories like that. But what does she actually think of this world?

She pauses for a moment, then gives you a small smirk. “I think we got here at just the right time,” Monika says, sounding like she’s letting you in on a secret. “The trip here wasn’t great, but once we saw what was going on here, I knew we could make something of it.”
You’re not sure what she means. She and the others, like any non-natives, really stick out on this planet, and they’re literally living underground. So how was Dhaal the right world for them?

Monika chuckles. “Little secret of the trade, [pc.name],” she says, and leans in closer to you. “Every world’s got an underground. Doesn’t matter if it’s a crazy-rich core planet or a tiny mining colony on some forsaken rock. Everywhere you go, you can find someone who’s set up on the wrong side of the law.” She pauses. “And then there’s places like Zhèng Shi, where there’s nothing but the wrong side of the law. But you get the idea.”
That’s definitely true. Even in Myrellion, where most of the civilization is literally the underground.

“I remember that,” Monika says with a nod. “We didn’t get to stay there long, but that’s a place that sticks with you.” She clears her throat. “But once we got a hold of what’s going on here on Dhaal, and how it works with the corps and all that, I knew we had a shot at slipping into this place’s underground. Dhaal works different from most places I’ve been, but... just look around.”
You glance around the bar, then look back at her, not sure what you’re supposed to be looking for. She and the others are running an illegal milk bar, you get that, but what does she mean?

“I mean this place needs an underground,” she says with a sly smile. “These people live under the corps’ thumbs, even if they feel like they don’t. So they need somewhere to get away from that, someplace where they don’t have to worry about whatever’s going on up in the city. And we give them that.”
And they provide something that pretty much all the zaika want, which doesn’t hurt at all.

Monika flashes you a grin. “Everybody’s got to eat,” she says, then nods over her shoulder to the cowgirl sharing the bar with her. “Not our fault one of us has what the zaika need. And lucky for us, they’re real eager to pay for it.”

Before you can ask Monika anything more, she turns to a zaika couple as they walk up to the bar, and pulls out two glasses. Looks like she’s done talking about this for now, but she might say more when she knows you better.

Before you can ask Monika anything more, she turns to a zaika couple as they walk up to the bar. She holds up a hand for you to wait, then pulls out two glasses and takes their orders. After pouring from two of the fancy-looking bottles behind the bar, she swipes their credit chits, then turns back to you as they walk away.
“Like I was saying,” she says, “business is good. And those two?” She leans in closer, and lowers her voice. “They’re from two rival gangs up in the city.”

You look over your shoulder at the two zaika as they return to their booth. Sure enough, the outfits they’re wearing match with two of the gangs you’ve seen in the city, and they’re definitely not in the same gang. But they seem fine with each other, friendly if not more. It doesn’t seem like that’s a common sight in the city.

When you turn back to Monika, she’s making the two credit chits dance back and forth across her fingers, flipping them from one side of her hand to the other. She realizes you’re looking at her, and flips her hand around, making the chits disappear.

“Every good pirate,” Monika begins, “knows you need neutral ground. You can’t fix everything by shooting it until it stops moving, though I’ve known lots of people who tried. But they know we’ll kick them out if they start shooting in here, so they don’t do it.”

That makes sense, though you guess it started out informally. Karrah didn’t disarm you when you came in, and you’re not the only one in here who’s fully armed, but you can see that nobody wants to get kicked out or banned from this place.

“And that’s why it works,” Monika says, nodding. “If one of the gangs had a place like this, or somehow the corps started one up as a front, you couldn’t have things like that.” She gestures to where the two gang members are talking and drinking. “So milk’s not the only thing we can give them.”
Have any of the gangs talked to her about that? If the gangs work here like they do pretty much anywhere else, you could see why one or more of them might think they could take over the bar.

Monika frowns a little. “It’s kind of strange now that I think about it, but that’s never come up,” she says. “Maybe Karrah scares them off, maybe they don’t want to ruin a good thing, maybe they actually want the neutral ground.” She shrugs. “But I’m good with it either way. Would be nice if it meant they didn’t try to jump us whenever we head up to the city, but you can’t have everything.”
Maybe they’re just trying to keep up appearances. As popular as the bar seems, you’d think the gangs would leave them alone by now.
“You might have noticed we’re not natives,” Monika says, then rolls her natural eye. “You know how it is out there. Just having nipples makes you a target.”

Before you can say anything else, Monika looks over your shoulder and frowns again. “Speaking of targets,” she says. “There’s someone I need to talk to.” She steps out from behind the bar, and heads over to one of the tables, then returns a moment later and says nothing more.
Looks like she’s done talking about that for now. If you ask her about this again when you’re better-known at the bar, she might have more to say.

Before you can ask Monika anything more, she turns to a zaika couple as they walk up to the bar. She holds up a hand for you to wait, then pulls out two glasses and takes their orders.
